old_claim.note: Contract Law provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Notarial registers preserved commercial agreements and legal acts, so contract law supplies the agreement and obligation framework that the records authenticated.

source_shape.source_locator: Chapter summary dates Giovanni Scriba's surviving notarial cartularies to 1155-64 and ties Genoese social encounters to commercial agreements.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source connects the earliest surviving Genoese notarial cartularies to a commercial-agreement environment.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A register that authenticates commercial agreements depends on the legal category of enforceable agreement rather than on generic recordkeeping alone.

ontology_before: Notarial records depended on contract law through a generic expert-inference edge.
ontology_after: Medieval notarial registers keep contract law as the source-backed legal-act framework for authenticated agreements.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: contract_law remains a direct enabling prerequisite, now source-backed.
would_reject_if: The node were broadened back to all written administrative records.
The cited source only mentioned paper or archives without commercial agreements.
Contract law were treated as a hard prerequisite for every kind of notarial act rather than enabling infrastructure for this scoped register tradition.
short_reason: Genoese notarial cartularies preserve commercial agreements, so contract law is a direct enabling framework.
